title: A coloured radiative cooling coating with high solar reflectance - Nature Energy
text:
Overcoming the trade-off between colouration and high solar reflectance is a challenge for radiative cooling materials. Now, an ethyl cellulose coating fabricated in one step achieves vibrant structural colours with negligible solar absorption and high long-wave infrared emission. This coating enables sub-ambient daytime cooling of up to 9 °C under sunlight.

ReferencesWang, T. et al. Colored radiative cooling: from photonic approaches to fluorescent colors and beyond. Adv. Mater. 37, 2414300 (2025). This review discusses coloured radiative cooling systems and compares their cooling performance and applications.Article 

Google Scholar 
Ma, X. et al. Fluorescence-enabled colored bilayer subambient radiative cooling coatings. Adv. Opt. Mater. 12, 2303296 (2024). This work demonstrates fluorescence-enabled coloured bilayer radiative cooling by using rare-earth-doped phosphors.Article 

Google Scholar 
Wang, T. et al. Innovative photon-engineered fluorescent tri-layer polymeric coatings for sub-ambient colored radiative cooling. Adv. Sci. 12, e11599 (2025). This study presents the design of fluorescent tri-layer polymeric coatings that use phosphors and Y2O3 nanoparticles to achieve Purcell-enhanced fluorescence emission for coloured radiative cooling.Article 

Google Scholar 
Chen, Y. et al. Colored and paintable bilayer coatings with high solar-infrared reflectance for efficient cooling. Sci. Adv. 6, eaaz5413 (2020). This study introduces a bilayer-coloured radiative cooling paint with a thin top layer that contains colourants that absorb visible light for colouration and a porous underlayer for solar reflection.Article 

Google Scholar 
Xue, X. et al. Creating an eco-friendly building coating with smart subambient radiative cooling. Adv. Mater. 32, e1906751 (2020). This study demonstrates fluorescence-enhanced solar reflectance and achieves sub-ambient daytime radiative cooling.Article
